items.title: New platform creates digital map for marine biobanks
items.summary: A new digital platform developed under the leadership of CIIMAR is making Portugal's marine biodiversity more accessible by bringing together thousands of biological resources into a single access point. The Blue Biobanks Digital Research Platform aims to bridge scientific research and industry

items.title: Collective agreements are least common where workers need them most
items.summary: Workers earning the lowest wages are the least likely to be covered by collective agreements in Germany, despite being the group for whom these protections are arguably most important. In 2021, only 34% of workers in the lowest wage decile were covered by collective agreements, compared with more th

items.title: Understanding anti-blackness at Hispanic-serving research universities
items.summary: At Hispanic-Serving Institutions (HSIs), conversations about diversity often center on supporting Hispanic/Latine students. New research from scholars at University of New Mexico highlights an important and sometimes overlooked issue--the experiences of Black students at these universities, specific

items.title: Geoscientists reveal how Earth's forces are shaping the 'Roof of the World'
items.summary: Geoscientists at the University of Glasgow have helped reveal new evidence about the formation of one of the highest mountainous areas on Earth—the Tibetan Plateau. A study by an international team of Chinese and U.K. geoscientists shows that the unique topography at the summit of the plateau is sha

items.title: To ancient astronomers, Theta Eridani was brighter for 1,000 years—now we know why
items.summary: There's a bit of a historical mystery surrounding the star Theta Eridani. Ptolemy in the second century A.D. and al-Sufi in A.D. 964 both recorded Theta Eridani as one of the 13 brightest stars in the sky. Hipparchus may have said the same. But there's a problem. For it to be one of the 13

items.title: New sensors capture warning signs before fish deaths in Lake Victoria
items.summary: Researchers from King's College London recorded the warning signs of a major low-oxygen event in Lake Victoria just hours before fish deaths were reported by local communities, demonstrating why earlier warning systems are urgently needed.

items.title: Dark energy flips its sign, but the Hubble tension refuses to budge
items.summary: For nearly a century, astronomers have known that the universe is expanding. In the late 1990s, two independent teams, the Supernova Cosmology Project, led by Saul Perlmutter, and the High-Z Supernova Search Team, led by Brian Schmidt and Adam Riess, discovered something strange: The expansion is sp

items.title: Children back group claims over evidence, but privacy reduces bias, experiments reveal
items.summary: As we move closer to Election Day 2026, voting preferences are moving back into focus—and with them, analyses of what drives partisanship at the polls. However, less frequently asked is when Americans show evidence of partisan behavior: shortly or well after reaching the legal voting age? As teenage

items.title: Why natural forests survive heat waves better than planted forests
items.summary: When a record-breaking drought and heat wave swept across China's Yangtze River Basin in 2022, forests across the region faced an extreme test. The event provided a rare opportunity for researchers to test how different forests respond when rising temperatures and water shortages strike at the

items.title: Early Career Faculty (ECF) 2025 Awards
items.summary: Back to ECF Home Advanced Diagnostics for High-Enthalpy Test Facilities Simulating Spacecraft Atmospheric Entry Planning for Autonomous Spacecraft Using Machine Learning Methods to Enable Onboard Guidance, Navigation, and Control

items.title: NASA Volunteers Help Zooniverse Reach 1 Billion Classifications
items.summary: The Zooniverse, a NASA grantee that runs the world’s largest platform for online people-powered research, has reached an extraordinary milestone: 1 billion classifications contributed by volunteers around the world. This milestone is a celebration of everyone who has marked a dip in a light curve, c

items.title: Moderate warming rewires one-third of microalga's genes, study finds
items.summary: Researchers have demonstrated for the first time that the microalga Chlamydomonas reinhardtii alters the activity of about one-third of its protein-coding genes in response even to moderate temperature changes. The study, published in the journal The Plant Cell, points to far-reaching consequences o

items.title: What is 'social media'? Study highlights public confusion about the term
items.summary: Research from Aston University has shown that the public has no clearly accepted definition for the term "social media" or agreement about which websites and platforms are classified as such.

items.title: Visible light triggers three-step cascade to make 3D drug-like molecules
items.summary: A team led by chemist Frank Glorius, a professor at the Institute of Organic Chemistry at the University of Münster, has developed a new light-driven reaction sequence. In this triple catalysis, one reaction step triggers the next like three dominoes in a row, toppling one after the other. The molec

items.title: Giant kangaroos survived until 6,500 years ago on the New Guinea coast
items.summary: Roughly 50,000 years ago, a kangaroo unlike any alive today lived in the mountain rainforests of New Guinea.

items.title: Dust in the wind: intense storms struck China, US in 2025, says UN
items.summary: China and the southern United States were hit last year by some of their worst sand and dust storms in decades, the United Nations said Friday.

items.title: New 3D COF structure could help tune porous materials for batteries and cleanup
items.summary: A research team synthesized and determined the structure of a borate-linked 3D crystalline covalent organic framework, TCTP-COF, via electron diffraction for the first time. These findings will help scientists determine the structure-property relationships for other 3D COFs and facilitate their tuni

items.title: Researchers find simple solution for extending the lifespan of LEDs made from glowing quantum dots
items.summary: A new study led by MIT researchers could drive the development of more energy-efficient digital displays—such as flat-screen TVs, augmented and virtual reality headsets, smartphone screens, medical imaging devices and even large-area ambient lighting surfaces—that also generate richer, brighter colo

items.title: Ancient DNA challenges family assumptions in medieval Scandinavian graves
items.summary: When archaeologists find adults and children buried together in medieval graves, it is often assumed that they were members of the same family. A new study from Stockholm University in Science Advances suggests otherwise.

items.title: Landslides kill 15 in Philippines as biggest typhoon in decades nears Taiwan
items.summary: Fifteen people were killed in landslides in the Philippines, and thousands in Taiwan were evacuated from their homes as the biggest typhoon in decades neared the region Friday.

items.title: Physical laws explain why most flies evolved similar flight, with mosquitoes as outliers
items.summary: A new study in PLOS Biology of 133 species of flies, mosquitoes and their relatives shows that most species fly in surprisingly similar ways. Physical and aerodynamic laws shape the evolution of their flight behavior more strongly than previously thought. Mosquitoes prove to be a striking exception.

items.title: Does multitasking ability really differ by sex? Not in the way you'd think
items.summary: Research simulates real-life multitasking performance to assess potential differences between men and women. When coordinating five different tasks, men ignored the conversational task more than twice as often as women, while showing similar performance to women in all other tasks.

items.title: Brazil deforestation hits new low in Amazon
items.summary: Deforestation in the Brazilian Amazon fell to its lowest level in a decade in the first half of the year, according to official figures released Friday.

items.title: China takes a page from SpaceX and recaptures the first stage of a rocket to reuse it
items.summary: China successfully recaptured the first stage of a rocket after a launch on Friday in a breakthrough for the country's space program, state media said.

items.title: Math reveals how honeybee hives balance the 'daring few, patient many' strategy
items.summary: How do bees make group decisions without a leader? Math experts have determined that the best strategy is for a few to assume the risk of foraging under all conditions while the majority stay safely back and forage only when conditions are favorable.

items.title: Tropical forests can switch from carbon sinks to carbon sources during El Niño
items.summary: Tropical forests draw down and store large quantities of CO₂ from the atmosphere. The Amazon rainforest in South America, for example, stores approximately 123 billion tons of carbon—more than is stored in any other terrestrial ecosystem in the world. But these forests are facing a critical challeng

items.title: Research identifies farming practices that improve irrigation efficiency
items.summary: Mississippi State scientists are building on two decades of irrigation research to identify production practices that help growers save water while improving crop yields.

items.title: New test certifies quantum measurements that simpler methods cannot mimic
items.summary: Proving that one quantum measurement is more powerful than another has long been difficult. Physicists from Heinrich Heine University Düsseldorf, Lund University and the University of Innsbruck have now developed and demonstrated a simple technique to certify that a certain class of measurements has

items.title: Artificial hand reproduces human gestures using memory written into light-responsive polymers
items.summary: Danqing Liu from Eindhoven University of Technology explores how interactions with digital systems can be improved through the sense of touch. To achieve this, she develops advanced liquid crystal polymers that respond to light. Her work has recently been published in two scientific journals, Scienc

items.title: High-status Iron Age burials reveal early links to Roman Empire
items.summary: A large cemetery containing the lavishly adorned remains of unidentified high-status individuals dating from around 2,000 years ago has been uncovered by archaeologists from Archaeology South-East (UCL Institute of Archaeology). The completed excavations near Chelmsford, Essex, revealed the cremated

items.title: NASA Photographer Captures Images from F-18 Over Washington
items.summary: NASA flight photographers capture history from a perspective few ever experience, getting a rare bird’s-eye view of the agency’s missions in action. Their photos document key NASA research and give the public a front-row seat to the work happening behind the scenes. Jim Ross, a photographer at NASA’

items.title: Report shows declining gun homicides, record gun suicides in 2024
items.summary: Nearly 45,000 people in the U.S. died by gun violence in 2024—one person every 12 minutes, on average—and an all-time high of 27,593 died by firearm suicide, according to the latest annual firearm mortality report from the Johns Hopkins Center for Gun Violence Solutions at the Johns Hopkins Bloomber

items.title: Researchers discover genetic secrets of mung bean crops
items.summary: Researchers at the Center for Crop and Food Innovation (CCFI) have made a significant contribution to a landmark study, uncovering tens of thousands of previously hidden structural variations influencing agriculturally important traits in the mung bean. The study, published today in Nature Genetics,

items.title: NASA space telescope maps magnetic fields of 'Lighthouse' pulsar
items.summary: For the first time, scientists have used NASA's IXPE (Imaging X-ray Polarimetry Explorer) to directly measure the magnetic fields of PSR J1101−6101, a pulsar located within what is often referred to as the Lighthouse Nebula. The results provide new insight into the structure of some of the most

items.title: Report finds the justice system in England and Wales is failing to meet people's needs
items.summary: The justice system in England and Wales is failing to meet people's needs, with cuts to legal aid forming part of a wider pattern of declining investment and support, warns a new UCL–led report. The findings present a stark picture of a system under strain, with austerity and sustained underfun

items.title: As national drought deepens, a new AI model helps balance water demands
items.summary: As drought strains water supplies across much of the United States, Virginia Tech researchers have developed an artificial intelligence (AI) model designed to help policymakers manage growing competition between agriculture and semiconductor manufacturing. Feras Batarseh, associate professor in the

items.title: Genomic study of the Asian house shrew reveals a complex history of Indo-Pacific trade and human migration
items.summary: Sometimes mistaken for a strange-looking mouse with a long, pointed snout, the Asian house shrew is a small, furry animal known for its musky odor. It's usually found lurking near homes and farms, ports and cities, across Southeast Asia, East Africa, southern Japan and islands across the Indian

items.title: New Jurassic dinosaur species identified in Thailand from a single bone
items.summary: A new study published in Scientific Reports describes the identification of a new species of long-necked dinosaur found in the Phu Kradung Formation in Thailand. The team calls the dinosaur Uragasaurus kalasinensis and says it is the first formally named mamenchisaurid dinosaur from Thailand.

items.title: Study links newspaper closures to distorted crime reporting
items.summary: Local newspapers serve as a vital check on public institutions, including law enforcement, according to new research from the University at Buffalo School of Management. Published in the American Journal of Criminal Justice, the study examines the societal cost of the ongoing decline in local journa

items.title: New model maps solar storms across 1 million miles around Earth
items.summary: A team at the Applied Physics Lab is working to understand the complex science behind predicting invisible threats that can quickly cripple electric grid infrastructure on Earth.

items.title: Waxing Gibbous Moon
items.summary: The waxing gibbous moon is nestled in the darkness of space in this June 26, 2026, image from the International Space Station. The space station was 264 miles above the Indian Ocean southeast of Madagascar at the time. The waxing gibbous phase comes before the full moon phase. During this time, the

items.title: Virtual tutoring: What started as a pandemic phenomenon has evolved from a temporary fix to an educational fixture
items.summary: When Amanda Neitzel first evaluated a live virtual reading-tutoring program called AirReading two years ago, she found what most educators would have predicted: modest results. One semester of live video tutoring nudged struggling early readers forward by roughly a month's worth of learning. Bu

items.title: Could exoplanets locked in eternal day and endless night support life?
items.summary: Ever so slightly bigger than Earth, the exoplanet LHS 3844b orbits its parent star, LHS 3844, a red dwarf 48.5 light-years from our solar system. Its rotational speed mirrors its orbital speed. The result? One side of LHS 3844b is perpetually bathed in scorching sunlight, locked into a never-ending,

items.title: Musk planira podatkovne centre u svemiru. Cilj je golema ideja iz 1960-ih
items.summary: AMBICIJE Elona Muska za budućnost SpaceX-a i čovječanstva temelje se na ideji začetoj 1960-ih, kada su astronomi počeli primati tajanstvene, nepoznate radijske izvore iz svemira.

items.title: What happened to Australia's snow season? A climate expert explains
items.summary: There's nothing like gliding down a snow-covered slope. That is, if you ask the thousands of people who make an annual pilgrimage to our alpine resorts during the Australian winter. But this year, the start to the snow season has been far from spectacular.

items.title: Znanstvenici razvili kontaktne leće koje same popravljaju ogrebotine
items.summary: KOREJSKI znanstvenici razvili su kontaktne leće koje se same obnavljaju pod UV svjetlom.

items.title: Hidden health risks found in New York City's free-roaming cats
items.summary: Cats may be cute and adorable, but stray and feral cats can sometimes pose a risk to human health. Veterinary researchers have discovered that more than 50% of free-roaming cats in New York City carry parasites that could potentially be transmitted to humans.

items.title: 10 fathers took extended leave to care for their babies—how their family life changed
items.summary: The UK government launched a major review of parental leave in July 2025, with recommendations expected in early 2027. One question sits at the heart of the review: What difference does parental leave make to family life?

items.title: Largest viral-protein library gives researchers new way to probe emerging pathogens
items.summary: To prevent viruses from sickening or killing us—whether it's an individual case of hepatitis B or a COVID pandemic—it's crucial to understand how the proteins they make initiate changes in our bodies that allow them to flourish. A new tool has broadened the scale at which researchers can s

items.title: Why bees are struggling to survive this summer
items.summary: You may be spotting dead bumblebees lying on pavements near you during the summer months. There are several reasons for this, some related to the weather and some very much due to humans.

items.title: If so many people live with toxoplasmosis, how dangerous is this brain parasite really? Should I be worried?
items.summary: About 1 in 3 people worldwide have been infected with a microscopic parasite called Toxoplasma gondii—or simply "toxo"—which can cause toxoplasmosis. After the initial infection, toxo settles into our muscles and brains, where it can remain for life.

items.title: Tiny mountain lakes pose big, overlooked flood risks, new study warns
items.summary: A new international study involving scientists from the University of Aberdeen has revealed a critical blind spot in global climate risk assessments—the growing danger posed by small alpine lakes formed by glacier retreat and permafrost thaw. Published in Nature Sustainability, the research highligh

items.title: New ligand engineering strategy creates more active nanocluster catalysts
items.summary: A joint research group from Tohoku University, Tokyo University of Science, Tokyo Metropolitan University and the Japan Fine Ceramics Center has developed a thermal catalyst that exhibits high carbon monoxide (CO) oxidation activity under low-temperature conditions.

items.title: Rising tides, rising tensions: New research calls for rethink of coastal law
items.summary: As sea levels rise and coastlines erode, Australia's legal system is struggling to keep up. Longstanding assumptions about who owns the coast—and who should pay when it disappears—are now at the center of growing disputes.

items.title: Warm Jupiter exoplanet transiting a sun-like star discovered
items.summary: An international team of astronomers reports the discovery of a new exoplanet orbiting a sunlike star as part of the Next Generation Transit Survey (NGTS). The newfound alien world, designated NGTS-39 b, is a Jupiter-sized planet with an equilibrium temperature of about 519 K. The discovery was deta

items.title: Study finds politically salient immigration issues can lead to higher industrial pollution
items.summary: A joint research team led by Professor Narae Lee from the School of Business and Technology Management at KAIST, in collaboration with Professor Heli Wang from Singapore Management University (SMU), analyzed immigration-related legislation and environmental data across the United States and found th

items.title: The biggest problem with solid-state batteries may finally be solved
items.summary: Researchers solved the mystery of how soft lithium dendrites crack the hard ceramic inside solid-state batteries, triggering short circuits. The breakthrough could help engineers build safer, longer-lasting batteries for smartphones, electric vehicles, and other electronics.

items.title: Bacteria turn dissolved uranium into stable compound in 130 days, study finds
items.summary: Researchers at the Helmholtz-Zentrum Dresden-Rossendorf (HZDR), together with Wismut GmbH and scientists from the University of Granada in Spain, have demonstrated for the first time that bacteria can convert uranium dissolved in water into a stable chemical compound when they have access to glycero

items.title: Peering into materials down to the nanoscale in the COCOON lab
items.summary: A new Tufts University imaging facility is doing something that most microscopy centers in the world cannot: allowing scientists to examine a butterfly wing, a living tissue or a microchip and reveal its physical structure, molecular chemistry and elemental composition across every scale, from the m

items.title: Assessing lab animals with AI
items.summary: Rutgers Office for Research (OfR) leaders collaborated with researchers around the world to develop an artificial intelligence (AI) program that has the potential to revolutionize lab research.

items.title: Astronomers reveal how clouds shape the hidden interiors of the galaxy's most common planets
items.summary: Sub-Neptunes are the most common type of planet in our galaxy. Bigger than Earth but smaller than Neptune, these worlds remain deeply mysterious because scientists still do not know what they are made of. What astronomers really want to figure out is their interior composition. They could be rocky p

items.title: This common pesticide may be quietly wiping out future bumblebees
items.summary: A next-generation pesticide designed to kill crop pests may also be interfering with the reproductive health of bumblebees. Researchers discovered that low-dose exposure to sulfoxaflor changed gene activity, especially in tissues involved in reproduction, raising concerns about long-term impacts on

items.title: Researchers link the mass extinction of once-dominant marine groups to intolerable heat, diminished oxygen in oceans
items.summary: A new Stanford-led study offers the clearest picture yet of how some ocean life survived our planet's biggest mass extinction while most animals did not. About 252 million years ago, 96% of marine species and 70% of land animals died off during the Permian–Triassic extinction event, known as th

items.title: Darwin's 150‑year‑old hillside steps mystery may have a new answer from virtual grazing animals
items.summary: Steep hillsides and mountainsides in many regions worldwide are often covered in characteristic step-like patterns, also known as terracettes. These repeating landforms have fascinated scientists for more than a century, yet the factors contributing to their formation had not been clearly confirmed

items.title: Europe's most active volcano may have a secret origin
items.summary: Mount Etna has long puzzled geologists because it doesn't fit any of the three classic ways volcanoes are thought to form. A new study suggests it may instead be fueled by ancient pockets of magma that are pushed upward through cracks created by shifting tectonic plates. If confirmed, Etna coul

items.title: Study underscores barriers for male survivor support
items.summary: Male survivors of intimate partner violence are less likely to seek help than female survivors, according to new research from Michigan State University's School of Social Work. As a result, these cases of intimate partner violence go unrecognized and underreported. To combat this, researchers

items.title: Nobelovac hrvatskih korijena: Hrvati su tvrdoglavi i govore što im je na pameti
items.summary: NOBELOVAC za fiziku John Martinis, porijeklom iz Komiže, posjetio je Hrvatsku. Govori o utjecaju hrvatskog nasljeđa na njegov rad, budućnosti kvantnog računarstva i inspiriranju mladih znanstvenika.

items.title: US pushes for weaker truck pollution rules
items.summary: President Donald Trump's administration on Thursday moved to loosen truck pollution regulations put in place by his predecessor, Joe Biden, in its latest environmental rollback benefiting makers of fossil fuel-burning vehicles.

items.title: H5 bird flu detected in Australian seabird for first time
items.summary: Scientists have detected the highly contagious H5 bird flu in an Australian seabird for the first time, the government said Friday.

items.title: Capturing the cosmic 'drift' before a star is born
items.summary: Stars like our sun are formed from the collapse of stellar objects called prestellar cores, cold and dense concentrations of gas and dust held together by gravity. While many questions remain about the exact mechanisms of star formation, advanced radio telescopes have given researchers new insights

items.title: Wally Funk, aviation pioneer who was the oldest woman to travel into space, dies at 87
items.summary: Wally Funk, an aviation pioneer who was the oldest woman to launch into space, has died. She was 87.

items.title: The galaxy’s coldest “stars” may actually be alien megastructures
items.summary: Scientists have identified new clues that could help astronomers spot one of the most famous hypothetical alien megastructures: a Dyson sphere. The study finds that red dwarfs and white dwarfs are the most promising stars to examine, since advanced civilizations could potentially build energy-harves
